note: Ladder rung 5 (final): EconomicJoint, the contender. The only policy that consults all three signals at once - lifecycle (retired => evict dead first), cost (recompute_cost, protect expensive-to-rebuild KV), and reuse (recency via 1/age). price(b) = 0 if retired else recompute_cost(b)/(age+1); evict cheapest-to-lose first. This is the last number the project owes.
provenance: Committed with results=TBD BEFORE any scored run (strict protocol; git timestamp proves prediction precedes number). The infrastructure commit that carries this file adds the policy, the per-kind cost extension, and unit tests, but NO scored panel numbers.
eval_design_settled_by_the_pressure_swee: Runs as a CURVE over the cap/T grid, not a single cell (the retired-cache sweep showed a single cell can land anywhere on an inverted-U). Two panels: (1) UNIFORM-cost parity panel - a check that EconomicJoint reproduces RetiredCache's curve, since under uniform cost the pricing term is inert by construction; (2) COST-SWEPT panel with heterogeneous per-kind cost, where pricing gets its only chance to show marginal value. Grid (all above the <1000-LRU-miss noise floor): cap/T in {0.33 (high-pressure anchor), 0.41, 0.45, 0.49, 0.53} (the band where retirement mattered, 0.53 the low-pressure edge of the measurable range).

cost_profile_decision.the_key_reversible_modeling_choice: At uniform block granularity a scalar recompute rate makes every block cost the same, so pricing has nothing to price; the cost-swept panel needs cost heterogeneity. Chosen source: per-KIND recompute cost, motivated by reconstruction provenance - some KV is cheap to rebuild, some expensive.
cost_profile_decision.rationale: reasoning and history must be REGENERATED by the model (full prefill cost, mult 1.0); tool_output is RE-FETCHABLE (re-run the tool or read a tool-result cache, mult 0.2); system_prompt is STATIC (reload from template, mult 0.05). So expensive = reasoning+history, cheap = tool_output+system. EconomicJoint protects the expensive kinds and evicts the cheap ones preferentially - a signal orthogonal to recency and lifecycle. Flagged as the reversible decision: if the profile is wrong the panel re-runs with another; the parity panel does not depend on it.

prediction_registered_before_run.parity_panel_hard: Under uniform cost EconomicJoint reproduces RetiredCache's curve EXACTLY at every cap (byte-for-byte scored cost), because price collapses to constant/age = recency with dead-first. If any cell differs, the lifecycle/recency wiring is a bug, not a finding.
prediction_registered_before_run.cost_swept_marginal_value_modest: Pricing should give a POSITIVE but MODEST marginal reduction in scored recompute cost vs RetiredCache under per-kind cost, by keeping expensive (reasoning/history) blocks resident that RetiredCache evicts by recency alone. Registered expectation: single-digit-percent scored-cost reduction over RetiredCache, concentrated in the critical pressure band (cap/T ~0.49-0.53) for the same reason retirement peaked there (dead/cheap-block occupancy is the binding constraint only near balance). I do NOT predict a dramatic win: the ladder's recurring lesson is that single orthogonal signals each collect a little and the bulk of the room is live-block reuse ORDERING, which pricing only partially addresses. Whether the third signal (cost), stacked on lifecycle+reuse, finally overtakes WA-LRU-tuned (the current best) is the open question; my registered guess is it does NOT dramatically overtake it, consistent with the pattern.
prediction_registered_before_run.falsifiable_edges: (1) parity panel not exact => wiring bug; (2) cost-swept marginal value ZERO or NEGATIVE => the cost signal is inert or harmful on this workload (a real finding: expensive blocks were not the ones under pressure, or protecting them cost more cheap-block misses than it saved); (3) a LARGE win (double-digit-percent over the field) => would refute the 'single signals collect little' pattern and is the outcome I am registering AGAINST.

panel1_uniform_cost_parity.verdict: PARITY IS NEAR, NOT EXACT - the hard prediction is REFUTED, but the residual is a legitimate size-pricing effect, not a wiring bug. EconomicJoint is 0.4-3.6% CHEAPER than RetiredCache even under uniform cost. Why: my prediction assumed uniform block size, but blocks vary (the trailing partial block of each prefix is smaller), so recompute_cost = size*rate is NOT constant at uniform rate; Econ's cost term therefore still prices size and prefers evicting small cheap blocks. The lifecycle+recency wiring IS verified correct (the curves are near-identical, dominated by dead-first+recency); the residual is the size term doing real, mild work. Not fixed, because pricing size is correct economic behavior; the parity claim was the thing that was wrong.

panel2_per_kind_cost.reading: EconomicJoint is the best policy at every cell EXCEPT the highest-pressure one (cap/T=0.33, where WALRU 158.0 still edges Econ 160.6). Its advantage is an inverted-U in pressure, peaking in the same critical band retirement did: the absolute scored-cost reduction over RetiredCache runs 2.5% -> 2.4% -> 18.3% -> 15.1% -> 5.7%, and in the band Econ beats cost-blind WALRU decisively (234.0 vs 283.8 at 0.45; 311.1 vs 466.1 at 0.49). Combining all three signals collects substantially more of the room than any one, but only where pressure is near-critical.

skeptical_caveat_on_magnitude: The percent-of-oracle GAPS under per-kind cost look enormous (Econ 311 vs WALRU 466 at 0.49) partly because the oracle itself exploits cost (it tie-breaks victims by lowest recompute cost), so under heterogeneous cost the oracle's scored cost drops and every ratio inflates - and a cost-aware policy (Econ) tracks the cost-aware oracle far more closely than a cost-blind one (WALRU). That is a FAIR comparison (the oracle is the shared target) but it amplifies the visual gap. The ROBUST measure is the absolute Econ-vs-RetiredCache scored-cost reduction (both lifecycle-aware, differing only in pricing): peak ~18% in the band, single-digit outside it. Report the ~18% as the headline marginal value of pricing, not the raw percent-of-oracle spread.
prediction_outcome: SPLIT, reported honestly. (1) Parity-exact (hard): REFUTED - near but not exact (0.4-3.6% cheaper), because I wrongly assumed uniform block size; the residual is legitimate size-pricing, not a bug. (2) Cost-swept 'modest single-digit, NOT a dramatic win over WALRU': RIGHT at high pressure (2.5% marginal, WALRU still ahead at 0.33), REFUTED in the critical band (18% marginal, Econ overtakes WALRU by ~50 points at 0.45). I under-predicted. Falsifiable-edge (3) - the outcome I registered AGAINST - fired: the JOINT policy delivers a large win in the band. Note this does NOT overturn the 'single signals collect little' pattern; it confirms its complement - it took ALL THREE signals together, and only near-critical pressure, to decisively move the needle.
thesis_disposition: The contender is vindicated, with the honest boundary conditions the whole ladder earned. EconomicJoint - the only policy pricing the full decision with lifecycle + cost + reuse - is the best policy in the critical pressure band (cap/T 0.45-0.53), cutting scored recompute up to ~18% below the best lifecycle baseline and beating the best signal-blind policy substantially. But the win is pressure-dependent (an inverted-U, marginal at high pressure where WALRU still leads) and depends on cost heterogeneity existing (the per-kind profile). This is the project's headline positive result AND its honest frame: not 'the economic policy wins,' but 'the economic policy wins where the room is unlockable, which is the near-critical band, and by combining the three signals no single policy uses.' The remaining unmeasured piece is Phase 4: real migrate costs would activate the offload/refuse actions this rung deferred, and real per-kind costs would replace the assumed profile.
cost_profile_reminder: The per-kind profile {reasoning/history 1.0, tool_output 0.2, system_prompt 0.05} is an ASSUMED model, the key reversible modeling choice, not a measured one. The direction of the result (pricing helps in the band) should be robust to the exact multipliers as long as cost is heterogeneous, but the magnitude is profile-dependent. Phase 4's measured costs replace it.
